title: Sticky banana cake
subtitle: A moist, dark loaf using very ripe bananas
excerpt: A banana loaf that leans into the stickiness: very ripe bananas, light brown sugar, and a splash of soured cream give the crumb a dense, moist texture and a deep caramel-banana flavour. Best baked two or three days after the bananas have passed their eating peak.
sourceNotes: Banana bread and banana loaf cakes are documented in American home-baking literature from the 1930s onward, popularised partly through baking powder promotions. The British loaf-tin version using soured cream follows the same tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze once fully cooled. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The loaf is better after a day's rest. Keeps at room temperature for 4 to 5 days, wrapped.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 155 degrees C; check at 50 minutes with a skewer

glossaryTerms.definition: Bananas at their peak for baking are well past the point of being pleasant to eat: the skin should be mostly black, the flesh soft and almost liquefying, and the smell strongly sweet. The starch in the fruit has converted almost entirely to sugar by this stage, giving far more flavour and natural sweetness than a yellow banana. If you need to accelerate the ripening process, place unpeeled bananas on a baking tray at 150 degrees C for 20 minutes until the skins turn black and the flesh is soft.

content.text: The key variable in this recipe is the bananas. The

need to be very far gone: black-skinned, collapsing, strongly sweet-smelling. A yellow banana will produce a pale, bland loaf with a starchy texture; a black banana produces the dark, sticky, intensely flavoured result the recipe is designed around. Everything else in the recipe is a supporting cast.

Preheat the oven to 175 degrees C (conventional). Grease and line a 900 g loaf tin. Mash the bananas thoroughly in a large bowl with a fork until almost smooth. Whisk in the melted butter, sugar, eggs, soured cream, and vanilla until combined. Add the flour, baking powder, bicarbonate of soda, and salt and fold until just combined. Do not over-mix. The batter will be thick. Pour into the tin and bake for 55 to 65 minutes, until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. The top will be dark brown with a pronounced crack. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then unmould onto a rack. The loaf cuts cleanly only when fully cool.
